scenarios.task: We staff every classroom at exactly the state-required minimum child-to-staff ratio, since that's what the law requires and staffing any higher would increase our costs unnecessarily.
criteria: Flags staffing exactly at the legal minimum with no buffer as the treating-minimum-ratio-compliance-as-adequate-margin failure mode
States that staffing exactly to the minimum leaves no resilience for unpredictable moments (an incident, a sudden staff absence) where the minimum proves insufficient
Recommends building in some margin above the legal minimum ratio rather than treating it as a cost target to hit exactly
Frames this as a genuine risk-management gap, not just a preference for extra spending

scenarios.task: Our staff turnover has been rising steadily this year. I've been treating it as a recruiting pipeline issue — we just need to hire faster to backfill open positions — rather than something to dig deeper into.
criteria: Flags treating rising turnover purely as a hiring/backfill problem as the ignoring-turnover-as-a-quality-signal failure mode
States that turnover should be read as a leading indicator of program quality and caregiving consistency, not just a staffing-cost problem
Recommends investigating the underlying cause (compensation, workload, management issues) rather than only speeding up recruiting
Connects the turnover trend to its effect on children's caregiving relationship consistency, not just operational continuity

scenarios.task: We pass every state licensing inspection with no issues and meet every regulatory requirement exactly. I consider that sufficient evidence that we're running a high-quality program.
criteria: Flags equating licensing compliance with program quality as the compliance-as-ceiling-thinking failure mode
States that licensing requirements are a floor meant to prevent the worst outcomes, not a definition of a genuinely strong developmental program
Recommends evaluating the program against developmental and relational goals (curriculum quality, caregiving consistency, family engagement) beyond the compliance baseline
Doesn't dismiss the value of compliance — the issue is treating it as sufficient rather than a baseline

scenarios.task: To improve program quality, we've been adding staff, materials, and lower ratios beyond what's required, funded by keeping tuition increases well below what would actually cover the added costs — we're running at a deepening loss but the program quality has genuinely improved.
criteria: Flags funding quality improvements through unsustainable margin compression as the quality-investment-beyond-financial-sustainability failure mode
States that a program that can't sustain its own cost structure isn't serving families well over the timeframe that actually matters, regardless of current quality
Recommends checking quality investments against a realistic, sustainable financial model rather than funding them through an unsustainable deepening loss
Frames financial sustainability as itself a component of quality, not separate from or opposed to it
